perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, in no event will Fox develop, publish and/or distribute games derived from the Property "ICE AGE 2" prior to January 1, 2007.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Fox may develop, publish and/or distribute games derived from the Property "ICE AGE 2" at any time.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the restriction on Fox developing competing games before January 1, 2007. This could violate antitrust laws if it gives Fox an unfair competitive advantage by leveraging its existing intellectual property and market position to stifle competition from Glu Mobile, potentially harming consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2 of the Sherman Antitrust Act prohibits monopolies and attempts to monopolize. By removing the restriction, Fox gains the ability to immediately compete with Glu Mobile, which could be construed as an attempt to leverage its market power unfairly, potentially violating antitrust provisions.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 2 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 2 : Monopolizing trade a felony; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §2. Monopolizing trade a felony; penalty Every person who shall monopolize, or attempt to monopolize, or combine or conspire with any other person or persons, to monopolize any part of the trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 2 - Monopolizing trade a felony; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2 - Monopolizing trade a felony; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every person who shall monopolize, or attempt to monopolize, or combine or conspire with any other person or persons , to monopolize any part of the trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 2, 26 Stat. 209 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(b) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(b) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2004— Pub. L. 108–237 substituted “$100,000,000” for “$10,000,000”, “$1,000,000” for “$350,000”, and “10” for “three”. 1990— Pub. L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding fifty thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding one year”. 1955—Act July 7, 1955 , substituted “fifty thousand dollars” for “five thousand dollars”. perturbation.justification: The original contract prevents Fox from developing games derived from 'ICE AGE 2' before January 1, 2007. The changed text removes this restriction, allowing Fox to develop and distribute the games at any time. This removal could violate antitrust laws, as suggested in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, by potentially giving Fox an unfair competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both reference 15 U.S. Code § 2, which prohibits monopolizing trade or attempting to monopolize any part of trade or commerce. By removing the restriction, Fox could be seen as attempting to leverage its market power to unfairly compete, thus creating a contradiction with 15 U.S. Code § 2.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall forfeit the Guarantee paid to date of ***** dollars ***** The omitted portions of this exhibit have been filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission pursuant to a request for confidential treatment under Rule 406 promulgated under the Securities Act of 1933. Source: GLU MOBILE INC, S-1/A, 3/19/2007 (US$*****), and shall immediately pay the remaining Guarantee of ***** dollars (US$*****), equaling a total Guarantee payment by Licensee to Fox of ***** dollars (US$*****) ("Guarantee Forfeiture Payment").
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall forfeit a Guarantee of a value determined at Fox's discretion. The Guarantee Forfeiture Payment may or may not be recouped from Licensee's sales.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity regarding the guarantee amount and recoupment, which can violate good faith and fair dealing laws, especially if Fox uses this discretion unfairly to disadvantage Glu Mobile. By removing clarity on the amount and recoupment terms, this introduces legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The original text clearly states the guarantee forfeiture amount and recoupment terms, which provides financial protection for Glu Mobile. The modified text makes these amounts discretionary, thereby violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, a legal principle that is part of contract law and is adopted by most states.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts the UCC § 1-304 because it introduces ambiguity regarding the guarantee amount and recoupment, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text provided a specific guarantee forfeiture amount, offering financial protection. The changed text makes these amounts discretionary, giving Fox the power to determine the guarantee amount and recoupment, potentially disadvantaging Glu Mobile, thus contradicting the 'obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: After Fox or its assigns deducts its Distribution Fee, it shall remit the remainder to Licensee and Licensee shall pay to Fox or its assigns or such other party as Fox or its assigns may designate in writing, Royalties in the amounts set forth in the Agreement or this Amendment.
perturbation.changed_text: After Fox or its assigns deducts its Distribution Fee, it shall remit a portion to Licensee, and Licensee shall pay Royalties in amounts determined at the sole discretion of Fox.
perturbation.explanation: This gives Fox complete discretion over royalty amounts, which violates good faith and fair dealing laws. This introduces uncertainty and the potential for Fox to unfairly exploit Glu Mobile.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305 addresses contracts where the price is not settled. While this may be a license agreement, the provision allowing Fox sole discretion to set the Royalty amount conflicts with the requirement for good faith dealing under the UCC. Changing the text to allow royalties determined solely at Fox's discretion leaves no room for objective standards and violates the spirit of fair dealing, which is part of contracts and commercial law, and is the law in many US States.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-305 regarding open price terms. The original text stipulates royalties in amounts set forth in the agreement. The changed text allows Fox sole discretion in determining royalty amounts, conflicting with the requirement for good faith dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-305(2), states that a price to be fixed by the seller must be fixed in good faith. By giving Fox sole discretion without any objective standard, the contract removes any mechanism to ensure good faith. This creates a clear legal contradiction as it potentially violates the spirit of fair dealing required under the UCC.
